coredata.dc:title: Monitoring biothiols dynamics in living cells by ratiometric fluorescent gold carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Xiaoxue
Peng, Zhenqi
Wang, Ziqi
Hua, Xinyi
Wang, Zhifang
Deng, Keqin
Yang, Xiumei
Huang, Haowen
Liu, Xuanyong
coredata.dc:description: Changes in biothiols, including glutathione (GSH) and cysteine, are closely related to a variety of diseases and cellular functions. Real-time monitoring intracellular GSH and cysteine dynamics in living cells are important for understanding pathophysiological processes. In this study, a stable heterostructure of dual-emission fluorescent gold carbon dots (GCDs) consisting of carbon skeleton and gold nanoclusters was prepared, which emits strong blue fluorescence and weak yellow-orange fluorescence. The blue fluorescence of the as-prepared GCDs has no response to biothiols including GSH and cysteine, but a turn-on yellow-orange fluorescence would be appeared accompanied by the biothiols reacting with the GCDs. Ratiometric fluorescent bioimaging therefore may be established based on the unique GCDs to detect GSH and cysteine levels in living cells. Moreover, real time monitoring GSH and cysteine levels in various cell lines in living cells at different growth stages was realized. The concentration of GSH in cancer cells is higher than that of normal cells, however, the level of cysteine in normal cells is consistently higher than in cancer cells at different stages of cell growth. The fluorescent GCDs probe provides a promising tool for tracking on regulation of GSH and cysteine dynamics by physiological environments due to the capability for real-time quantitation of GSH and cysteine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescent bioimaging
Glutathione
Cysteien
Biothiols dynamics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of GSH and cysteine in human serum
Preparation and characterization of fluorescent GCDs
Responses and selectivity of GCDs to thiol-containing compounds under physiological conditions
Bioimaging of GCDs in living cells
Detection of GSH and cysteine in human serum
Imaging and detecting GSH and cysteine in living cells
Monitoring ultratrace changes of GSH and cysteine in living cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption (black line) and photoemission (red line, under excitation at 310 nm) spectra of GCDs (Abs: absorption spectrum, Ex: excitation spectrum, Em: emission spectrum), inset shows photographs of an aqueous solution of GCDs under visible light (left) and UV irradiation (λ = 365 nm). (b) XRD pattern of GCDs. HRTEM image (c), EDS (d), HAADF-STEM (e) and ABF-STEM (f) images of GCDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

HRTEM (a), HAADF-STEM (b) and ABF-STEM (c) images of etched GCDs, (d) excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of etched GCDs, (e) scheduled structure (I, II) of GCD consisted of carbon skeleton and gold nanoclusters. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Turn-on response of fluorescent GCDs at 570 nm along with increasing concentration of GSH, (b) fluorescence intensity ratio (ΔF570/F435) vs concentration of GSH: the equation of calibration curve was ΔF570/F435 = 1.517 + 0.01925c with a linear coefficient R2 = 0.9761, (c) selectivity of GCDs probe for thiol-containing compounds, biothiol-free human serum and some metal ions, (d) fluorescence intensity ratio (ΔF570/F435) vs concentration of cysteine: the equation of calibration curve was ΔF570/F435 = 1.3733 + 0.03844c with a linear coefficient R2 = 0.9993.

(a) Bioimaging of HIBEpiC incubated with GCDs for 2 h in the blue channel (emission from 440 to 500 nm) and red channel (emission from 540 to 600 nm), (b) imaging ability of GCDs to cells including HIBEpiC (up) and REB (bottom), (c) fluorescence intensity from parallel experiments was recorded by microplate reader, and (d) cell toxicity of the GCDs to cancer cells and normal cells. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Additive experiments by adding the known amounts of GSH (final concentration 4.76 mM) and cysteine (final concentration 0.476 mM) to the biothiol-free human serum, (b) recovery experiments of fluorescent GCDs towards cysteine, (c) concentrations of GSH and cysteine in serum samples detected by the GCDs fluorescent probe.

(a) Interference tests on the intracellular macromolecules to fluorescent GCDs. Discriminative detection of biothiols in living cells: (b) represents normal cells HIBEpiC, b1 shows bioimaging of intracellular GSH and cysteine, b2 displays bioimaging of intracellular cysteine of cancer cells; (c) represents cancer cells REB, c1 is bioimaging of intracellular GSH and cysteine, c2 is bioimaging of intracellular cysteine, (d) the levels of cysteine and GSH in living cells.
Monitoring changes of fluorescence in HIBEpiC (a) and REB (b) at different growing stages.

Effect of cell growth in vitro on the intracellular GSH and cysteine: (a) The number of cells varies with the culture time, (b) comparison of changes of GSH concentration in both normal and cancer cells at different stages of cell growth, (c) comparison of changes of cysteine concentration in both normal and cancer cells at different stages of cell growth.

head.ce:title: Monitoring biothiols dynamics in living cells by ratiometric fluorescent gold carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

Hunan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of High Performance Ceramics and Superfine Microstructure

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of High Performance Ceramics and Superfine Microstructure, Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai 200050, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Changes in biothiols, including glutathione (GSH) and cysteine, are closely related to a variety of diseases and cellular functions. Real-time monitoring intracellular GSH and cysteine dynamics in living cells are important for understanding pathophysiological processes. In this study, a stable heterostructure of dual-emission fluorescent gold carbon dots (GCDs) consisting of carbon skeleton and gold nanoclusters was prepared, which emits strong blue fluorescence and weak yellow-orange fluorescence. The blue fluorescence of the as-prepared GCDs has no response to biothiols including GSH and cysteine, but a turn-on yellow-orange fluorescence would be appeared accompanied by the biothiols reacting with the GCDs. Ratiometric fluorescent bioimaging therefore may be established based on the unique GCDs to detect GSH and cysteine levels in living cells. Moreover, real time monitoring GSH and cysteine levels in various cell lines in living cells at different growth stages was realized. The concentration of GSH in cancer cells is higher than that of normal cells, however, the level of cysteine in normal cells is consistently higher than in cancer cells at different stages of cell growth. The fluorescent GCDs probe provides a promising tool for tracking on regulation of GSH and cysteine dynamics by physiological environments due to the capability for real-time quantitation of GSH and cysteine.

ce:section.ce:para: Intracellular biothiols, including glutathione (GSH), cysteine and homocysteine, play an important role in physiological and pathological processes, disease prevention [1]. GSH, a mercaptotripeptide, functions as an antioxidant, which is of many essential roles in biological processes maintaining the redox homeostasis and intracellular metabolism [2–4], dynamic redox equilibrium [5,6] and signal transduction [7]. Abnormal GSH levels are associated with a variety of diseases, such as cancer [8,9], liver disease [10,11], neurodegenerative diseases and aging [12,13]. Cysteine and homocysteine are linked with the redox homeostasis in vivo [14,15]. Cysteine deficiency involves some syndromes such as slow growth in children, hair depigmentation, liver damage, skin lesions and so on [16–19]. Due to the close connection with different diseases, it is necessary to monitor these biothiols with selective and sensitive methods.

Recent years, quantities of strategies were established to detect thiol-containing biomolecules [20–22]. Fluorescent sensors based on small molecule biothiol compounds have attracted great attention and developed rapidly due to the unique advantages of high specificity, low detection limits, and bioimaging in vivo [23,24]. Many developed fluorescent probes focused on the qualitative analysis of GSH in human serum and intracellular biothiols [25,26], and some methods have been proposed to directly quantitatively analyze cysteine or homocysteine [27–29], whereas, studies on quantitative detection of biothiols, particularly, monitoring changes of thiol-containing biomolecular levels are relatively rare. It is significant to develop selective or simultaneous approaches for monitoring changes of GSH and cysteine to understand disease mechanisms and find potential treatments based on the dynamic link between biothiols and diseases. However, there is a challenge to explore effective and reliable methods for monitoring the changes of GSH, cysteine or homocysteine, because of cysteine, homocysteine having similar molecular structure and reactivity to GSH [30]. Researchers have been pursuing design and synthesis of intelligent fluorescent probes for the detection and imaging of biothiols. A number of organic fluorophores have been synthesized to detect the biothiols [31–33], however, photobleaching limits the use of these fluorophores for long-term assays. Alternatively, different fluorometric techniques based on nanomaterials have been used for detecting biothiols [34,35]. As a class of fluorescent nanoparticles, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn increasing attention in quantifying trace amount of biothiols due to their unique advantages such as highly stable fluorescence intensity, good resistance to photo- and chemical degradation, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [36,37]. However, fluorescent sensors based on nanomaterials are often disturbed by other complex backgrounds. Fortunately, self-calibration of externally interfering fluorescence intensity can be realized by ratiometric fluorescent probes. Ratiometric fluorescence can provide intrinsic built-in corrections for environmental effects, and therefore has advantages in improving sensitivity and accuracy, leading to more efficient detection both in vivo and in vitro systems [38–41].

In this study, a stable heterostructure of dual-emission fluorescent gold carbon dots (GCDs) consisting of carbon skeleton and gold nanoclusters embedded in the about 2 nm composite was prepared, which emits strong blue fluorescence and weak yellow-orange fluorescence. The blue fluorescence of the as-prepared GCDs has almost no response to GSH and cysteine, however, another fluorescent peak emitting yellow-orange fluorescence appears accompanied by the biothiols reacting with GCDs, Thus, the GCDs can provide a ratiometric fluorescence for detection GSH and cysteine, particularly, GSH and cysteine can be distinguished under certain conditions. Compared to most known CDs exhibit emission in the blue and green regions, and there are few reports of CD-based fluorescence ratio sensors in living cells, a ratiometric fluorescent GCDs for detection of intracellular GSH and cysteine was established by measuring the fluorescence ratio of F570/F450. Moreover, ultra-trace changes of GSH and cysteine levels could be monitored in normal and cancer cells at different growth stages. The results show that the concentration of GSH in cancer cells is larger than normal cells during the whole cell growth stages; however, it was observed that the normal cells have higher cysteine level than cancer cells. The GCDs fluorescent probe provides a promising tool for tracking on how to regulate GSH and cysteine dynamics by physiological environments due to the capability for real-time quantiﬁcation of GSH and cysteine.

GCDs were prepared by microwave heating. 1.5 mL of a 0.1 M GSH solution was slowly added to 5 mL of a 20 mM freshly prepared HAuCl4 solution in Erlenmeyer flask, then the mixed solution was diluted to 50 mL with double distilled water (Lead R&D, China). Subsequently, 2 g of glucose was added to the solution by stirring to obtain a clear solution. The flask was placed in a domestic microwave oven (Glanza, China) and heated by microwave (500 W) until the solution evaporated to dryness. After that, 50 mL of double distilled water was added to the flask to dissolve GCDs and the resulting brown solution would be centrifuged at 10,000 rmp for 10 min (Cence H1850). Finally, the obtained supernatant was dialyzed with a dialysis tube (molecular weight: 8000 to 14,000) against water for 24 h to obtain GCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH and cysteine in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum samples were obtained from volunteers at a local hospital and stored at −20 °C until further analysis. Preparation of the standard curves: Gold nanospheres were first added to the human serum for 8 h to fully react the biothiols in the serum with the gold nanospheres, and biothiol-free serum was obtained by centrifuged to precipitate gold nanoparticles for removing the conjugated biothiols. Then, varying concentrations of GSH were added to the biothiol-free serum to prepare GSH standard solutions with different concentrations. 100 μL of the GSH standard solution was added to 2 mL of GCD solution for reacting 20 min. The fluorescence spectrum of the resulting mixture solution measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer excited at 380 nm, and the corresponding fluorescence intensity values at 435 and 570 nm were collected. The standard curve of GSH was acquired from the relationship between the fluorescence ratio ΔI570/I435 and the concentrations of GSH. The standard curve of cysteine was obtained in a similar manner.

Human serum samples were diluted 100-fold with 10 mM PBS solution, and then two identical serum samples were taken for subsequent experiments. One of which 100 μL human serum sample was directly added to 2 mL GCDs solution, and the mixture was vortex-mixed for 15 min (Vortex Mixer QL-866) and then incubated for 10 min at room temperature. Another was added to 100 μL oxidizing solution obtained by adjusting the pH of 30% H2O2 to 5.5 for reacting 20 min, which was added to 2 mL of the GCDs solution for incubation of 15 min. The fluorescence spectra were collected under excited at 380 nm.

Cell viability was tested in 3 replicates by the alamarBlue Cytotoxicity Test (AbD Serotec Ltd, UK). 100 μL of medium containing 5000 cell seeds (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was added to each well in a 96-well plate, and the cells were incubated at 37 °C., 5% CO2 for 24 h. After the medium was removed carefully from the wells, 100 μL of GCDs with different concentrations were added to the wells of a 96-well plate so that their final concentrations were 38, 76, 152, and 304 μg/mL, respectively. The fluorescence emitted at 590 nm was recorded using a microplate reader (Bio-Tek, ELK800) under excited at 380 nm. Cell viability was calculated according to the alamarBlue assay and expressed as the reduction ratio of alarmarBlue.

Cells were seeded onto the culture dishes with a density of 6 × 106 cell/dish and the culture medium was added. After incubation for 1Day, 2Day, 3Day, 4Day, 5Day, the culture medium was replaced by 4 mL GCD solution. Two hours later, the culture dishe was rinsed with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) for three times, placed into a flask containing 4 mL PBS, and observed under confocal fluorescence microscopy (Leica, SP8, Germany) excited at 405 nm (blue channel from 440 to 500 nm, red channel from 540 to 600 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of fluorescent GCDs
ce:section.ce:para: GCDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted method. Fig. 1 a shows the spectra curves of the as-synthesized GCDs, and the maximum absorption wavelength is at 280 nm in the visible-ultraviolet range. The fluorescence spectrum illustrates that a strong fluorescence peak at 435 nm was found excited at λ = 380 nm, and another weak shoulder appeared about 570 nm. The brown yellow GCDs aqueous solution displays an intense blue color irradiated by UV light (Fig. 1a inset). The characterization of XRD presented in Fig. 1b shows the XRD pattern displaying two broad peaks centered at 2θ = 20° and 32° originated from disordered carbon atoms. Fig. 1c shows the high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of the as-prepared GCDs (around 2 nm) with the lattice fringes, similar to the carbon dots (CDs) reported by previous works [42–44]. These results suggest that the prepared GCDs are a kind of typical CDs.

The elemental Au was identified by the energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) presented in Fig. 1d, which appears to contradict the XRD pattern that the GCDs are typical CDs only containing elemental carbon. Further investigation was carried out by scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) to characterize the GCDs. The Z-contrast nature of the high-angle annular dark field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) imaging is an ideal technique for differentiating heavy (such as gold, Z = 79) from light atoms (such as carbon, Z = 6) because it is hard to see light atoms with this characterization. HAADF-STEM and annular bright-field scanning transmission electron microscope (ABF-STEM) images are shown in Fig. 1e and f. They indicate that in some GCDs, the Au atoms are ordered arranged in crystal form (1 1 1), while others are present in GCDs as amorphous Au atoms in the microstructure of the as-prepared GCDs.

The weak fluorescence peak at 570 nm shown in Fig. 1a might originate from the Au nanoclusters in the GCD microstructure, but does it come from the surface or internal structure of GCDs? Further investigation was carried out and the GCDs were etched by aqua regia. The resulted nanoparticles were characterized by TEM presented in Fig. 2 a, which shows similar morphology to the as-prepared GCDs displaying a well-ordered flat (0012) face. HAADF-STEM and ABF-STEM images presented in Fig. 2b and c indicate the etched GCDs have similar microstructure to the as-prepared GCDs. However, the corresponding excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra presented in Fig. 2d show the shoulder peak at 570 nm disappeared, suggesting that the fluorescent Au nanoclusters were etched and the etched GCDs exhibit typical fluorescent property of carbon dots (CDs). A comparison of STEM-HADDF and STEM-ABF images between Figs. 1 and 2 indicates that the contrast in etched GCDs is not as obvious as that of the as-prepared GCDs. In the HADDF, it can be seen that the surface of the etched GCDs have fewer gold atoms compared to the as-prepared GCDs, but more amorphous carbon with a relatively shallow contrast. Therefore, it is speculated that the surface of etched GCDs might be enwrapped by carbon layer with well-ordered flat face after the Au atoms were removed by the etched reagent, resulting in the contrast unclear with the background. It also suggests that the surface of nanoparticles is carbon enriched, and the Au atoms are distributed inside and the surface of the GCDs. To sum up, GCDs are considered to be a type of composite nanoparticles exhibiting the properties of both carbon dots and gold nanoclusters. A stably heterogeneous structure of the GCD is scheduled in Fig. 2e in two forms of microstructure (I or II), integrated with carbon skeleton and gold nanoclusters in the composite, and the gold nanoclusters distributed on the surface of GCDs are responsible for the fluorescence at 570 nm. Meanwhile, the stability of the gold nanoclusters on the surface of the GCDs was explored. The fluorescence of free gold nanoclusters would be immediately quenched with the addition of Na2S2O3 (Fig. S1 in supplementary material). The fluorescence arisen from the gold nanoclusters on the surface of GCDs would not disappear within 20 min. This means that the gold nanoclusters distributed on the surface of the GCDs are protected to some extent by the carbon layer of the GCD surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Responses and selectivity of GCDs to thiol-containing compounds under physiological conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of the GCDs to GSH was first investigated. A significant enhancement of fluorescence intensity centered at 570 nm was observed with the addition of GSH to GCDs. As noted in Fig. 3 a, it is clear that a gradual increase of fluorescence intensity occurred along with increasing concentration of GSH. In contrast, no fluorescence at 570 nm was observed upon the addition of GSH to the etched GCDs. It further proves that the fluorescence at 570 nm originates from the gold nanoclusters on the surface, rather than the gold nanoclusters inside the GCDs, and the enhanced fluorescence emission is the result of the GSH reacting with the gold nanoclusters distributed on the surface of the GCDs. The fluorescence peak at 435 nm is arisen from carbon skeleton, which has no fluorescence response to GSH. As the fluorescence peak at 570 nm acts as reporter, the blue emission peak at 435 nm almost remains constant, indicating that the fluorescent GCDs may serve as ratiometric fluorescence. Thus, the GCDs can be used as ratiometric fluorescence for detecting GSH to obtain high signal-to-noise ratio when a large amount of other substances are present. The fluorescence ratio (ΔF570/F435) vs concentration of GSH was plotted as Fig. 3b, in which a linear relationship was observed in the concentration range of 0.016–190 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) for GSH was calculated to be 4.7 nM (3σ/K). Considering the fact that the concentration of GSH in human serum and cells is in the range of 1–15 mM, GCDs may act as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for quantitative analysis of GSH in diluted biological samples.

Cysteine was also investigated and it was observed that the fluorescence of the GCDs increased as the increase of concentration of cysteine, suggesting that the thiol group is responsible for fluorescence enhancement. To validate this hypothesis, thiol-containing compounds, such as 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid (MUA) and homocysteine were evaluated. As expected, the fluorescence at 570 nm was significantly enhanced after the addition of these mercapto-compounds other than homocysteine to GCDs, as shown in Fig. S2. It confirms that GCDs can be used as specific recognition element for the determination of thiol-containing compounds, with the exception of homocysteine, which has turn-on fluorescence response signal at 570 nm. To estimate the selectivity of this fluorescent probe for detection of biothiols, it is necessary to test some metal ions and biological molecules in practical samples which might coexist with biothiols. Cations such as Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Co2+, biothiol-free human serum were evaluated. Biothiol-free human serum (without GSH, cysteine) was obtained by the reaction of biothiols in human serum with gold nanoparticles, and then the nanoparticles conjugated with biothiols were removed by centrifugation from human serum. GCDs exhibit negligible variations of fluorescence intensity in two emission bands upon addition of other substances, including biothiol-free human serum. Fig. 3c shows that the cations of these essential trace metal elements in the human body have almost no fluorescence response. Although the biothiol-free human serum is very complex, containing some cations and a larger number of biomolecules such as proteins and small molecules, these results imply that GCDs are high selectivity for biothiols (including GSH and cysteine) in human serum.

Similarly, the response of the fluorescent GCDs to cysteine was investigated under the same experimental conditions. A perfect linear relationship between increasing fluorescence ratio (ΔF570/F435) and the increase of concentration of cysteine was obtained, as shown in Fig. 3d. It indicates that the fluorescent GCDs may be utilized to quantitatively detect cysteine in human body fluids in view of the wide concentration range of cysteine with this protocol. In addition, the stability of GCDs in the presence of GSH was investigated. TEM images shown in Fig. S3 illustrate that the GCDs exhibit good stability in the presence of GSH (10 mM) and the particle size of GCDs did not change compared to the as-prepared GCDs.

The mechanism of fluorescence enhancement might be related to the thiol group bound to the gold nanoclusters on the surface of GCDs. Due to the use of GSH in the preparation of GCDs, the gold nanoclusters on the surface of the prepared GCD were partially bound by thiol groups, but the surface of the gold nanocluster were not saturated and leave vacant sites to be reacted, which forms a defect-like structure and hinders efficient fluorescence emission [45]. Once the vacant sites are occupied by additional thiol ligands, the fluorescence intensity will increase significantly. Considering that the sizes of most proteins (such as BSA 3-D size of 14.0 nm × 4.0 nm × 4.0 nm) [46] is larger than a single GCD nanoparticle (about 2 nm), the thiol groups on the protein molecules cannot bind to GCD due to steric hindrance, indicating the protein thiol groups do not interfere with the fluorescence of the GCDs.

The specific structure of homocysteine in aqueous solution affect the reaction between the homocysteine and fluorescent GCDs. Homocysteine chemistry has two distinctive features compared to cysteine. First, the additional carbon in the homocysteine side chain allows it to exist as a stable five-membered cyclic thiolactone (homocysteine thiolactone) [47]. Second, homocysteine easily forms disulfides with free thiol groups was confirmed by several research groups [48,49]. These characteristics result in fewer free thiol groups in the homocysteine solution. In this way, the fluorescent GCDs exhibit poor sensitivity to homocysteine. This suggests the homocysteine cannot be detected in biological samples using this GCDs-based probe, and would not interfere with the detection of GSH and cysteine in the biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging of GCDs in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the potential applications of GCDs in bioimaging, the cell imaging capability was investigated. Human intrahepatic biliary epithelial cells (HIBEpiC) were incubated with GCDs for 2 h and then imaged using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) at 405 nm laser excitation. Fluorescence in the blue and red channels was collected. The dark-field, bright-field, and overlay images of the HIBEpiC incubated with GCDs are shown in Fig. 4 a, in which the cells exhibit a remarkable red emission in addition to blue fluorescence. In comparison, apparent blue fluorescence imaging was directly observed from GCDs by CLSM presented in Fig. S4. It shows that the red channel is dim and it is hard to find the fluorescence under the same condition. These results indicate that the significantly enhanced red fluorescence is originated from biothiols reacting with GCDs.

The imaging of GCDs to other cells was investigated. Regardless of whether it is in normal cells (HIBEpiC) or human cholangiocarcinoma cell line RBE, a significant enhancement of fluorescence signal was observed over time and substantially stable after 60 min, as shown in Fig. 4b. Meanwhile, another type of cancer cells (human osteosarcoma cell, MG63) was also tested, which further demonstrates that the GCDs have good membrane permeability. In addition, a set of parallel experiments was carried out for collection of fluorescence intensity recorded by microplate reader (Fig. 4c). These results indicate that GCDs can be used to image both normal cells and cancer cells, and they are suitable for detecting GSH and cysteine in living cells. The cytotoxicity of the GCDs was determined by the alarmarBlue assay. Both cancer cells and normal cells were employed to test the cytotoxicity of GCDs. GCDs are non-toxic to both types of cells when the concentration of GCD is less than 76 μg/mL, as shown in Fig. 4d, which indicate that the as-prepared GCDs is low toxicity and relatively bio-safety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH and cysteine in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: Although fluorescent GCDs can sensitively detect GSH or cysteine separately, the biological system contains both GSH and cysteine. To explore the possibility of GCDs for the detection of GSH and cysteine in biological samples, the first challenge is how to separately detect GSH and cysteine in human serum and cells before conducting the experiments to separately monitor GSH and cysteine in cells. Fortunately, further experiments manifest that GCDs have no fluorescence response to oxidized GSH, as shown in Fig. S5a, which may provide a possibility to distinguish GSH and cysteine in biological system. It was observed that GSH could be rapidly oxidized to form oxidized GSH by H2O2 in the absence of GCDs under slightly acidic environment; however, cysteine will not be affected under the same conditions. Meanwhile, effect of the order of reagents added to the reaction system on fluorescence of GCDs was investigated (Fig. S5b). If GSH is first oxidized by H2O2 and then added to GCDs solution, no obvious fluorescence enhancement was observed. However, if GSH is first added to GCDs, the fluorescence would increase significantly, and the subsequent addition of H2O2 would not cause any change in fluorescence intensity of GCDs. These observations illustrate two issues: 1) the thiol group in the compounds may interact with GCDs to occupy the vacant sites of defect-like structure, which enhances the fluorescence of GCDs, and the added H2O2 would not oxidize GSH immobilized on the surface on GCDs; 2) once the GSH is oxidized by H2O2 to form a disulfide bond, it can no longer form an Au–S bond with the gold atoms on the surface of GCDs. This also further verifies the reasonability of proposed mechanism on this fluorescent probe.

Subsequent investigation on human serum samples was carried out to quantitatively analyze GSH and cysteine. In fact, the measured fluorescence intensity is the sum of GSH and cysteine in the serum sample. To identify the accuracy of this strategy, the additive fluorescence experiments were performed by adding the known amounts of GSH and cysteine to the biothiol-free human serum, as shown in Fig. 5 a, which demonstrates that the fluorescence in serum is the sum of GSH and cysteine. In addition, cysteine was chosen to investigate the feasibility of this method. Different amounts of cysteine were added to the biothiol-free human serum for recovery experiments and the detailed results are shown in Fig. 5b. The recovery of cysteine in the diluted sample was between 96.4% and 104.9%, which meets the requirement of the quantitative analysis in real biological samples.

The detection of GSH and cysteine was performed as follows: firstly, the fluorescent spectrum of the serum sample was collected and the total fluorescence intensity of the serum was recorded. Secondly, another identical human serum sample was oxidized by H2O2 in acidic media, and the fluorescence measured come from cysteine in the serum. The difference in fluorescence measured is the fluorescence intensity of GSH. Finally, the concentrations of GSH and cysteine in the serum can be calculated by the standard curves, and the results summarized in Fig. 5c were in accordance with previous methods [50–52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging and detecting GSH and cysteine in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the separated detection of GSH and cysteine in human serum with spectral response of the GCDs to biothiols, the ability to image and detect intracellularly in normal and cancer cells was further investigated using CLSM. Prior to detection, the interference of macromolecules in the cell was investigated. The cells were first disrupted by repeated freezing and thawing, and then the broken cells were dialyzed to separate small molecules. The remaining cell residues were then added to GCDs, as shown in Fig. 6 a. The coincident fluorescence curves indicate that other substances in the cells do not interfere with the detection of biothiols. Two identical groups of cells were taken in the following study. Firstly, one of which HIBEpiC cells were incubated with the GCDs for 2 h at 37 °C then directly imaged. Images of HIBEpiC were collected from both channels upon excitation at 405 nm. As shown in Fig. 6b, fluorescent probe-treated HIBEpiC cells show bright fluorescence in the blue channel and red channel. The bright red fluorescence indicates that GCDs reacted with cellular GSH and cysteine, producing strong fluorescence. Meanwhile, another group of cells was treated with an excess of H2O2 solution (pH = 6) for 1 h, which reacted with all of the free GSH within the cells to convert it to oxidized GSH. Subsequently, the same amount of free GCDs as the group one were added and incubated with cells. As expected, the fluorescence emission in the red channel was much weaker arisen from only cysteine reacting with GCDs in the cells, while no significant change was observed in the blue channel. From the bright-field image, we found that the cells were viable throughout the bioimaging experiments. On the other hand, similar experiment was carried out toward the cancer cells (REB), as shown in Fig. 6c. These results are similar to the detection of GSH in human serum, in which the difference of measured red fluorescence comes from GSH. Therefore, GCDs are able to distinguish GSH and cysteine in both normal and cancer cells, indicating that the GCDs can be potentially applied to detect GSH and cysteine in living cells. Furthermore, from the bioimaging of the cells in Fig. 6 after oxidation by H2O2, an interesting finding was observed that the fluorescence of the red channel in normal cells is brighter than that of cancer cells, implying that the level of cysteine in normal cells is greater than that of cancer cells, and further experimental verification will be performed in the following study.

Although discrimination of GSH and cysteine in living cells is possible using the bioimaging, it is difficult to acquire the accurate levels of GSH and cysteine from these images. To accurately detect levels of biothiols in living cells, microplate reader was employed to quantitatively analyze GSH and cysteine. Taking account of the complexity of the cellular composition, standard addition method was adopted to quantify cysteine and GSH in this study. Firstly, varying concentrations of cysteine were added to the cell solution and incubated for 1 h at 37 °C, and then the fluorescence intensity was recorded using microplate reader. It is found that the fluorescence intensity gradually increases with increasing concentration of cysteine in the medium. The plot of fluorescence intensity as a function of concentration (Fig. S6a) illustrates that there is a linear relationship between cysteine concentration and fluorescence response. Similarly, GSH can be quantitatively detected according to the linear relationship displayed in Fig. S6b. The levels of cysteine and GSH in normal cells and cancer cells are summarized in Fig. 6d. From the table, it can be seen that the concentration of GSH in cancer cells is greater than that of normal cells, which is consistent with the previous reports [53–56]. However, the results indicate that the concentration of cysteine in the two types of cells is exactly the opposite of GSH, namely, the concentration of cysteine in normal cells is significantly greater. The interesting finding is the first observed that the concentration of cysteine clearly drops after cell carcinogenesis, contrary to the change of GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring ultratrace changes of GSH and cysteine in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Both normal and cancer cells were investigated using bioimaging. Firstly, growth-related GSH and cysteine contents in growing HIBEpiC in vitro were studied over a period of 4 days. Before imaged, these cells were incubated with GCDs about 2 h at 37 °C. All images show fluorescence in the blue and red channels, as shown in Fig. 7 a. Along with the growing cells, the GCDs in cells exhibit changes in red color fluorescence, in which the fluorescence intensity firstly gradually increases and then decreases after two days, but no obvious change in blue channel could be observed. This result suggests that the levels of biothiols varied with the growth of cells. Similarly, the changes of biothiols in cancer cells were monitored displayed in Fig. 7b.

The exact levels of GSH and cysteine and growth-related parameters in growing cells in vitro are shown in Fig. 8 . Both normal and cancer cells first grew exponentially, and then the growth rate slowed down (Fig. 8a). GSH concentration in both normal and cancer cells changed when different stages of cell growth were compared, as shown in Fig. 8b. When the number of cells actively proliferated, the GSH levels increased in the cells, reached a maximum at about 48 h, then, as the total number of cells increased and the growth rate decreased, the GSH concentration gradually decreased over the next two days. Clearly, the concentrations of GSH in cancer cells are always greater than that of normal cells throughout the growth of the cells. Similarly, in the initial stage of proliferation, the levels of cysteine in the cells increased, reaching a maximum after 48 h of incubation, but with the number of cells increased, and the levels of cysteine gradually decreased over the next two days. Finally, the levels of cysteine basically returned to the starting value by the 72 nd h, as shown in Fig. 8c. Contrary to GSH, in the process of cell growth, the concentrations of cysteine in normal cells are always higher than those in cancer cells.

In summary, dual-emission GCDs consisting of carbon skeleton and gold nanoclusters were synthesized to detect biothiols concentrations including GSH and cysteine in human serum and living cells. Not only did we successfully achieve quantification of GSH and cysteine in human serum, but also monitoring of GSH and cysteine levels in various cell lines in living cells at different growth stages. The results show that the concentration of GSH in cancer cells was higher than that in normal cells, which was consistent with previous studies. Furthermore, we found that the level of cysteine in normal cells is significantly higher than that in cancer cells. The results presented here indicate that the GCDs probe is a promising tool for tracking on how to regulate GSH and cysteine dynamics by physiological environments, because they have the ability to quantify GSH and cysteine in real time.

Under supervision of Prof. Haowen Huang and Prof. Xuanyong Liu Xiaoxue Xie completed the main experimental work, and the rest of experimental work was completed by Zhenqi Peng, Ziqi Wang, Xinyi Hua, Zhifang Wang, Keqin Deng and Xiumei Yang. This paper was written by Professor Haowen Huang.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled “Monitoring and quantification of biothiols dynamics in living cells at different growth stages by ratiometric fluorescent gold carbon dots”.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675049), National Natural Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholors of China (51525207), Science and Technology commission of Shanghai Municipality (19JC1415500).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selectively monitoring glutathione in human serum and growth-associated living cells using gold nanoclusters
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Xie, Z. Peng, X. Hua, Z. Wang, K. Deng, X. Yang, et al., Selectively monitoring glutathione in human serum and growth-associated living cells using gold nanoclusters, Biosens. Bioelectron. 148(2020) 111829.
